title: Incentivizing Organ Donation Under Different Priority Rules: The Role of Information
abstract: This paper examines the incentive to register for deceased organ donation under alternative organ allocation priority rules, which may prioritize registered donors and/or patients with higher valuations for organ transplantation. Specifically, the donor priority rule grants higher priority on the organ waiting list to those who have previously registered as donors. The dual-incentive priority rules allocate organs based on donor status, followed by individual valuations within the same donor status, or vice versa. Both theoretical and experimental results suggest that the efficacy of the donor priority rule and the dual-incentive priority rules critically depends on the information environment. When organ transplantation valuations are unobservable prior to making donation decisions, the hybrid dual-incentive rules generate higher donation rates. In contrast, if valuations are observable, the dual-incentive priority rules create unbalanced incentives between high- and low-value agents, potentially undermining the efficacy of the hybrid dual-incentive rules in increasing overall donation rates.

author.Mengling Li: MOE Key Laboratory of Econometrics, Department of Economics, School of Economics, Wang Yanan Institute for Studies in Economics, Fujian Key Laboratory of Statistics, Xiamen University, Fujian 361005, China
author.Yohanes E. Riyanto: Division of Economics, School of Social Sciences, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore 639818, Singapore

article.1. Introduction: The scarcity of human organs for transplantation is a major concern for numerous countries worldwide. Despite the persistent efforts of governmental bodies and healthcare institutions to raise awareness about organ donor registration, the gap between the number of donors and the number of patients seeking a transplant continues to widen each year (Howard 2007,Cameron et al. 2013,Goldberg et al. 2015,Bernstein 2016,Cherry 2017).1Organ donation and transplantation offer significant benefits to both individuals and society, including decreased medical expenses, preservation of countless lives, and increased life expectancy. Addressing the shortage of transplantable organs and narrowing the growing gap between donors and patients is of utmost importance in promoting social well-being. As monetary incentives and transactions are deemed controversial and illegal in most countries (Becker and Elias 2007;Roth 2007,2018;Elias et al. 2015;Gordon et al. 2015;Healy and Krawiec 2017), efforts to boost the organ donation rate must be based on nonfinancial means. This paper explores how to incentivize deceased organ donation through the design of organ allocation priority rules. In general, organs for transplantation can be obtained from both living and deceased donors. Living donors donate an organ, such as a kidney, while still alive, with little adverse impact on their bodily function, whereas deceased donors provide multiple organs for transplantation at the time of death. The latter are the predominant source of organ donation in practice. For instance, in the United States, deceased donors provided 85.3% of the transplants performed in 2020.2 A well-conceived organ allocation policy has the potential to mitigate the scarcity of transplantable organs by improving the allocation process and providing stronger incentives for donation.3In practice, organ allocation policies are primarily determined by priority rules, which dictate the order in which patients receive organs. In many countries, the ranking of these priorities is based on factors such as waiting time, compatibility of the donor and recipient’s immune systems, and the benefit to the recipient’s survival (Bertsimas et al. 2013). In addition to the routine medical criteria for allocation priority, organ waiting lists in some countries, including Singapore, Israel, Chile, and China, consider each patient’s donor status and grant priority to individuals who were previously registered donors (Iyer 1987,Lavee et al. 2010,Zuniga-Fajuri 2015,CODAC 2018).Kessler and Roth (2012)refer to such a policy as a donor priority rule and provide the first analysis on the improvement in donor registration rates after the introduction of such a priority rule. Using empirical data,Stoler et al. (2016,2017) document the positive impacts of implementing donor priority in Israel, reporting a 39.1% increase in the deceased donor registration rate from 1992 to 2013. In this paper, we analyze allocation priority rules that account for donor status and/or the heterogeneous valuations of patients for organ transplantation both theoretically and experimentally. Controlled laboratory experiments enable us to extrapolate the qualitative and directional effects of alternative allocation policies, even in cases where empirical data are lacking because these policies have not yet been implemented (Smith 1976).4In this regard, this approach provides us with an opportunity to examine the interactive effects of the donor-priority incentive and other common factors featured in priority design, as well as the efficacy of introducing the donor priority rule on top of existing allocation policies in countries such as the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada.5 Our main objective is to investigate whether hybrid dual-incentive priority rules that take into account of both the donor status and the value of a transplant can further boost donation rates and alleviate organ shortages. Although not explicitly mentioned in policy disclosures, a value-based priority rule can exist in various forms in practice.6For instance, value can refer to the inherent survival benefit or quality-adjusted life years (QALYs), which can be objectively verified by a central planner based on medical records. In this regard, value-based priority rules essentially seek to “improve outcomes for transplant recipients, increase the years recipients may have a functioning transplant, and increase utilization of available organs” as stated in a policy note by the OPTN.7More detailed discussions about the alternative interpretations of value and rationales for considering value-based priority rules are provided inSection 2.1. In the context of organ donation and allocation, it is possible that donors may or may not be aware of the value of a transplant for them. To address this situation, we first establish a theoretical framework of organ donation and allocation that considers two kinds of information environments, depending on whether agents have knowledge of their own valuations when making a donation decision. We then compare the aggregate incentives for donation under various allocation priority rules that incorporate both donor status and/or individual value for organ transplantation.8Specifically, theValue Priority Ruleprioritizes patients on waiting lists based on the value they place on receiving an organ transplant. TheDonor Priority Ruleprioritizes patients who have previously registered as deceased organ donors. Additionally, two hybrid priority rules based on a dual-incentive approach are considered. TheDonor+Value Priority Ruleassigns organs based on donor status first and then on value within the same donor status group, whereas theValue+Donor Priority Ruleassigns organs based on value first, followed by donor status within the same value-type group. In our model, agents face a basic tradeoff between a thick and a noncongested market. If donor status does not influence the organ allocation priority, only agents with a high degree of altruism register to donate. However, by incorporating donor status into the allocation process, more agents become incentivized to register for donation and attain a higher priority on waiting lists, thereby accessing a larger pool of organs. This additional incentive exists as long as the marginal increase in priority ranking suffices to offset the costs of donation, thereby fostering a thick market for organ supply. As the number of donors increases, the demand for organ allocation congests the market, thereby reducing the marginal benefit of registering as an organ donor. Consequently, when making donation decisions, each agent must consider several factors, including the cost of donation, their individual value for organ allocation, the supply of organs, and the exclusivity of the priority queue. Our theoretical analysis highlights the role of information in the relative performance of different priority rules. We show theoretically that when agents do not know their individual valuations before making their donation decisions (referred to as theunobservedvalue environment), the dual-incentiveDonor+Value Priority Rulealways dominates the other three allocation rules in terms of the aggregate donation rate (seeProposition 1). In contrast, theDonor Priority Ruleoutperforms both dual-incentive priority rules in theobservedvalue environment under certain regularity conditions (seeProposition 2). We additionally find that in scenarios with severe organ shortages, the two hybrid dual-incentive rules are equally effective in promoting organ donation. To gain further insight into how individual behavior is influenced by allocation rules and information environments, we conduct laboratory experiments to examine the organ donation incentives of human subjects. To mimic the extent of organ shortages worldwide, our experimental setup mirrors the scenario of severe organ shortages in our theoretical model. In this case, our theoretical analysis establishes the equivalence between the two dual-incentive priority rules. Accordingly, our experimental treatments vary based on the three allocation priority rules, namelyValue,Donor,andDonor+Value, and two information environments, that is,observedandunobserved. In the (un)observed value environment, subjects are (not) informed about their individual valuations for receiving B-unit allocations before making their donation decisions. Our experimental results align well with our theoretical predictions. Across both information environments, we observe that theValuetreatment results in the lowest proportion of donors among all examined allocation rules. This outcome is intuitive because there is no additional advantage to becoming a donor. More intriguingly, the comparison between theDonor+ValueandDonortreatments critically depends on the information environment. In the unobserved value environment, theDonor+Valuetreatment proves superior, but this result is reversed in the observed value scenario. Further analysis of the donation incentives among high- and low-value subjects highlights that these results stem mainly from the incentive distortions in the observed value environment. When subjects are informed of their valuations, we observe an imbalance in incentives between the high- and low-value subjects. However, in the unobserved value environment, such incentive distortions are absent. In the observed value environment, high-value subjects, who benefit more from their inherent survival advantages, are more likely to donate. However, this reduces the incentives of low-value subjects, particularly in theDonor+Valuetreatment, as the contributions of low-value subjects benefit high-value subjects first. Low-value subjects gain access to the supply only after the demand of all the high-value subjects (with the same donor status) has been satisfied.9 The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. InSection 2, we introduce the institutional background and review the related literature.Section 3presents the model and theoretical results.Section 4outlines the experimental design, andSection 5discusses the experimental results. We conclude with further discussions inSection 6.
article.2. Institutional Background and Related: In this section, we examine the concept of value in the context of organ donation and transplantation and provide justifications for its consideration in the priority rule. We highlight the various forms in which value manifests in the official practices in different countries. Furthermore, we provide a review of the related literature and emphasize our key contributions. 2.1. Interpretations of Value The allocation of donated organs and placement of patients on the waiting list in the United States are guided by three ethical principles, namely, utility, justice, and respect for persons (NASEM 2017). The principle of utility calls for evaluating, measuring, and comparing the benefits and harms of an allocation policy, which can often be measured through the QALY metric. This factor can be regarded as the value within our context. Similarly, value can also be considered in terms of the inherent survival benefit, a common consideration in lung and kidney allocations in many countries, including the United States, Australia, and China (Bertsimas et al. 2013,NHMRC 2016,CODAC 2018). The survival benefit essentially measures the number of years a candidate on the waiting list is likely to be able to benefit from an organ transplant, as represented by measures such as the lung allocation score (LAS) for lungs and the estimated posttransplant survival (EPTS) and kidney donor profile index (KPDI) for kidneys. Thus, a priority-based allocation policy using these scoring systems resembles the value-based allocation rules analyzed in our study.10 Interpretations of value based on QALY or survival benefit indicate that individuals are unlikely to know their valuations prior to making a donation decision, especially if they are young and healthy. Nevertheless, there are some occasions where the value or at least part of it can be determined beforehand.11For instance, if we interpret the value as life expectancy or blood type, this information can generally be obtained from medical records. All else being equal, an individual with a longer life expectancy has a higher value than someone with a shorter life expectancy (Howard et al. 2015,Genie et al. 2020).12With regard to the blood type interpretation, blood type O individuals have inherently lower valuations due to difficulties in finding compatible organs (Glander et al. 2010). A similar interpretation of value based on blood type and organ size is presented byErgin et al. (2020)in the context of liver exchange. Anecdotal evidence also supports our assertion that value-based priority rules are present, although not explicitly stated in the guidelines.Harhay et al. (2018)find that early diagnosis and early recommendation for placement on the waiting list are prevalent among privileged patients with better access to primary healthcare and insurance. As another example, there are substantial variations in regulations for transplants and the intensity of the organ shortage problem across regions in the United States (Kong et al. 2010,Ata et al. 2016,Stewart et al. 2018). Empirical evidence suggests that patients can advance their position on the waiting list for transplants by strategically relocating to regions with favorable regulations and lesser severity of organ shortage in the United States (Cha 2015,The Associated Press 2015,Smith 2017,Shah et al. 2018). Overall, the previous discussions suggest that a value-based allocation rule is explicitly or implicitly recognizable in various practices.13For expositional brevity, we refer to these different interpretations simply as value throughout the paper. Although a setup where the value is unknown at the time of donation decisions seems to be more realistic, it would be interesting to analyze the alternative where value is known in advance, at least as a counterfactual information scenario, from a practical policy perspective in terms of information disclosure.14Motivated by these facts, both our theoretical analysis and experimental design distinguish between the observed and unobserved value environments. 2.2. Literature Review Many studies have explored the design of waitlist mechanisms and incentive rules to moderate the scarcity of transplantable organs and promote organ supply (Howard 2007,Kessler and Roth 2012,Eames et al. 2017,Ata et al. 2020,Dai et al. 2020,Sönmez et al. 2020,Agarwal et al. 2021,Kim et al. 2021,Li et al. 2023). Our study is closely related to the seminal work byKessler and Roth (2012). Using both theory and laboratory experiments, they find that an organ allocation policy that prioritizes registered donors significantly outperforms a policy that does not consider donor status. They also conjecture that a hybrid dual-incentive priority rule exploiting both donor status and value in organ allocation can potentially incentivize even more donations. In this paper, we formally analyze the desirability of these dual-incentive priority rules in different information environments by means of both theory and laboratory experiments. The pioneering work ofKessler and Roth (2012)subsequently inspired growing interest in the use of laboratory and field experiments to study alternative ways to improve organ donation rates, includingKessler and Roth (2014a),Li et al. (2013,2022,2023),Lacetera et al. (2014),Li (2016), andHerr and Normann (2016,2019), and many others.15These previous experimental studies have focused on homogeneous valuations of receiving organ donations among patients. However, recent empirical studies byAgarwal et al. (2021)andAta et al. (2020)find substantial heterogeneity in patients’ value of kidney transplants based on structural estimations and highlight its importance in evaluating alternative waitlist and allocation mechanisms. Accordingly, our study contributes to this line of experimental research by examining the efficacy of alternative designs of priority rules that explicitly account for the heterogeneous valuations of patients for organ transplantation. Furthermore, we investigate the role of information about individual valuations in assessing the relative performance of these priority rules. The theoretical literature on deceased organ donation decisions is growing but relatively scant in comparison with the number of experimental studies.16Dai et al. (2020)incorporate heterogeneity in health risks to examine the welfare consequences of the donor priority rule.Kim et al. (2021)extend the donor priority benefits to one’s family members and analyze allocation priority rules based on these extended family benefits.Kim and Li (2022)consider the interactive effects of the donor priority rule and matching barriers related to blood type compatibility. Unlike the previous studies, our paper introduces heterogeneity in the value of organ transplants and considers the type-specific and aggregate donation incentives under different priority rules, which are designed based on the heterogeneous value and/or the donor status of agents. Our explicit consideration of heterogeneous value in organ allocation is related toSu and Zenios (2006)andDai et al. (2020).Su and Zenios (2006)model agents’ value as their private information about the expected improvement in quality of life after transplantation. The organ supply is exogenously formulated in their study, instead of being endogenously determined as in our model. InDai et al. (2020), the value of each agent is captured by the expected total quality-adjusted life expectancy, which follows fromSu and Zenios (2006)and is observable by each agent before making the donation decision, similar to our observable value environment. The priority rules considered in their study depend solely on the donor status without accounting for the heterogeneous value. Different from these previous studies, our analysis covers both the observed and unobserved value environments, thereby capturing a wider range of real-world settings. Moreover, we explicitly formulate and analyze value-based allocation priority rules in addition to the donor priority rule. Finally, in addition to the dimension of priority rules, considerable attention in the economics literature has focused on the design of default rules.17Many studies document that opt-out policies have a positive effect on deceased donor registration rates; see, for example,Johnson and Goldstein (2003),Abadie and Gay (2006),Li et al. (2013),Shepherd et al. (2014),van Dalen and Henkens (2014), andUgur (2015). Nevertheless, more recent literature reveals increasing concerns over and criticisms of implementing opt-out policies (Fabre et al. 2010,Sharif 2018,Arshad et al. 2019,Glazier and Mone 2019), which does not significantly increase the actual donation rates compared with opt-in policies (Costa-Font et al. 2021) and may even result in a reduction in the living donation rate (Becker et al. 2022). Hence, our study focuses on the opt-in regime, which is less controversial and less likely to have unintended consequences for the alternative source of organ supply.
article.3. Theoretical Framework and Predictions: This section considers a parsimonious and stylized model of organ donation and allocation by building on the theoretical framework inKessler and Roth (2012).18Our main purpose is to compare donation incentives under different organ allocation rules in different information environments. We discuss the welfare implications in Online Appendix D. 3.1. Basic Setup The model consists of two periods. In the first period, a continuum of agents simultaneously decide whether to register for deceased organ donation. We use𝑖∈{𝑑,𝑛}i∈{d,n}to denote donor status, wheredrepresents “donor” andnstands for “nondonor.” Agents incur heterogeneous costs of registration. The direct cost consists of administrative logistics, the discomfort of thinking about one’s own death, and the fear that doctors may not work as hard to save registered donors (Teresi 2012).19Meanwhile, an agent may derive direct benefits from registering as an organ donor, for instance, the feeling of altruism (Prottas 1983). Combining all these factors, we formulate the net cost of donation for each agent, denoted byc, as independently and identically distributed with a smooth cumulative distribution function (CDF)F(c). To rule out the trivial equilibrium in which no one registers as a donor, we assume that𝐹⁡(0)>0F⁡(0)>0such that the donation rate is always positive regardless of the allocation priority rules (Baughn et al. 2006,López et al. 2018). In the following analysis, we refer tocsimply as the donation cost for descriptive convenience. In the second period, there are two mutually exclusive health shocks. First, an agent may die from brain death with probability𝛽∈(0,1)β∈(0,1). In this case,𝛼>0α>0organs are made available to the public organ pool if this agent registered as a donor in the first period. The second possible health shock is organ failure with probability𝜃∈(0,1)θ∈(0,1), in which case an agent requires an organ transplant. An agent with organ failure, or simply a patient, may or may not receive an organ donation. The organ allocation outcome depends on the donation decisions of the other agents and the allocation priority rule, which are discussed inSection 3.2.20Otherwise, an agent remains healthy. Our analysis focuses on an organ shortage scenario, where the maximal organ supply rate is insufficient to meet the aggregate organ demand rate, that is,𝛼⁢𝛽<𝜃α⁢β<θ, similar to the assumptions inSönmez et al. (2020)andKim et al. (2021). This assumption is also largely consistent with the stylized facts observed from organ donation and transplantation statistics in many countries.21 The individual valuation of receiving an organ donation is denoted byVj, with𝑗∈{ℎ,𝑙}j∈{h,l}representing an agent’s value type. The proportion of agents with high valueVhis𝛿∈(0,1)δ∈(0,1). We consider two information environments that represent different levels of uncertainty with respect to alternative interpretations of value. Specifically, our analysis distinguishes whether agents know their own valuations at the time of their donation decisions, a setting that we refer to as theobservedvalue environment, or do not know their valuations, which we call theunobservedvalue environment. At the time of organ allocation, most of the uncertainty about value (if any) is resolved in the case of medical need. For analytical tractability, we assume that each agent’s value and donation cost are independent. The payoffs of agents are determined by their donation decisions, health statuses and organ outcomes. If an agent with organ failure does not receive a donation, the individual payoff is normalized to 0. If an agent needs an organ and receives one, the payoff is equal to individual valuation for the organVj. For the remaining states, the payoffs are unrestricted because they never enter into an agent’s donation decision problem. 3.2. Allocation Priority Rules We consider allocation priority rules that account for the organ donor status and/or individual valuation. Each priority rule essentially specifies a ranking of the endogenously determined probabilities of receiving a donation for each type of agent, denoted byPij, with𝑖∈{𝑑,𝑛}i∈{d,n}and𝑗∈{ℎ,𝑙}j∈{h,l}, which also measure the survival rates for patients with organ failure. Specifically, our theoretical analysis focuses on the following four priority rules. (1)Value Priority Rule. The allocation priorities are determined by only the value of the agents; those who have a higher value for organ allocation are given higher priority. Hence, the probabilities of receiving an organ for different types of agents are ranked as (2)Donor Priority Rule. Only donor status matters for allocation priorities. The organs are first rationed among all registered donors. Only after demand from donors is fully satisfied do nondonors have a chance to receive an organ allocation. This indicates (3)Donor+Value Priority Rule. This hybrid dual-incentive rule assigns allocation priorities based on donor status first, followed by the value type within the same donor status group. It essentially merges (1) into (2). The priority tiers are therefore ranked as (4)Value+Donor Priority Rule. Similar to (3), this hybrid rule essentially merges (2) into (1). The allocation priorities are determined first by the value type and then within the same value type by the donor status of the agents, which yields Overall, the priority rankings under the first two rules are coarser than those under the dual-incentive priority rules. For each of these priority rules, the endogenous organ supply determinesPij, which feeds back into the individual donation incentives. 3.3. Theoretical Predictions To compare the equilibrium donation rates, we use𝜇𝜏𝑟μrτto denote the donation rate under priority rule𝑟∈{𝑣,𝑠,𝑠⁢𝑣,𝑣⁢𝑠}r∈{v,s,s⁢v,v⁢s}, wherevandsstand for theValue Priority Ruleand theDonor Priority Rule, respectively, and theDonor+Value Priority Ruleis denoted bysv, whereas theValue+Donor Priority Ruleis labeled byvs, and𝜏∈{𝑢,𝑜}τ∈{u,o}represents the information environment with unobserved and observed value, respectively.22 Based on the equilibrium characterizations, it follows that theValue Priority Ruleresults in the lowest aggregate donation rate among the four priority rules, regardless of the information environment. This is intuitive because introducing donor status into the allocation rule provides positive priority benefits and enlarges the donation incentives for donors of at least one value type. However, whether the hybrid dual-incentive rules, which exploit both donor status and value in organ allocation, would further leverage priority design to improve donation rates is not obvious, especially in the observed value environment. This is mainly because the dual-incentive rules result in unbalanced incentives between high-value and low-value agents, as shown inEquations (5)and (6). Under the dual-incentive priority rules, with four tiers of priority ranking inEquations (3)and (4), the marginal benefit of donor registration varies with the degree of organ shortage in equilibrium. Specifically, in the case of a severe shortage, the demand of patients in the first priority tier cannot be fully satisfied, which indicates𝑃𝑑⁢ℎ∈(0,1)Pd⁢h∈(0,1). Otherwise, we havePdh= 1, referred to as the case of mild or moderate shortage. Therefore, the equilibrium outcome can be broadly classified into two cases depending on whether the demand of the high-value donors can be fully satisfied. In the following, we first consider the scenario with unobserved valuations, followed by the observed value environment. Proposition 1states our first theoretical prediction about the relative donation incentives in the unobserved value environment, which indicates that theDonor+Value Priority Ruleis always more effective than theDonor Priority Rulein terms of incentivizing organ donation. Notably, these results are general in the sense of imposing minimal assumptions on the distribution of donation costs. Both allocation rules generate a higher donation rate than that of theDonor Priority Rule. The intuition is as follows. The hybrid dual-incentive rules create a more exclusive priority club to share scarce organs than does theDonor Priority Rule, such that the high-value donors have the privilege of first accessing donated organs. Under the dual-incentive rules, each agent in the first priority tier has a higher chance of receiving a donation than do their counterparts under theDonor Priority Rule. With value type unobserved at the time of the donation decision, each agent regards oneself as a high-value type with probabilityδ. Therefore, the exclusive privilege for high-value donors under the dual-incentive rules attracts more donations. The scenario with the observed value is more complicated because there are two cutoff costs in equilibrium. The following proposition suggests that it is possible for theDonor Priority Ruleto strictly dominate the dual-incentive allocation rules.23This stands in sharp contrast to the previous result in the unobserved value environment. (Comparison with Observed Value).Consider a scenario where each agent knows individual value type at the time that the donation decision is made. In the case of a severe organ shortage, the aggregate donation incentives under each priority rule satisfy To provide intuition for this result, we first note that the aggregate donation rate in the observed value environment can be decomposed into two additive parts as𝜇𝑟=𝛿⁢𝐹⁡(𝑐∗ℎ,𝑟)+(1−𝛿)⁢𝐹⁡(𝑐∗𝑙,𝑟)μr=δ⁢F⁡(ch,r∗)+(1−δ)⁢F⁡(cl,r∗). In the case of a severe shortage, an immediate observation is that the dual-incentive priority rules are equivalent to each other. Compared with theDonor Priority Rule, the dual-incentive rules enlarge the degree of incentive distortions with𝑐∗ℎ,𝑟>𝑐∗ℎ,𝑠ch,r∗>ch,s∗and𝑐∗𝑙,𝑟<𝑐∗𝑙,𝑠cl,r∗<cl,s∗for𝑟∈{𝑠⁢𝑣,𝑣⁢𝑠}r∈{s⁢v,v⁢s}. In other words, the high-value agents are more incentivized under the dual-incentive rules than are their counterparts under theDonor Priority Rule, whereas the low-value agents are less willing to donate. Intuitively, in the presence of a severe organ shortage, donated organs are exclusively shared among high-value donors, whereas low-value donors have no chance of receiving an organ allocation. As a result, whether the aggregate donation rate under theDonor Priority Ruleexceeds that under the dual-incentive rules crucially depends on which of these two forces dominates. The first part ofProposition 2suggests that when the cost distribution is concave, and the difference betweenVhandVlis relatively small, the aggregate donation rate under the hybrid dual-incentive rules is lower than that under theDonor Priority Rule. Under these conditions, each dual-incentive rule disincentivizes low-value agents more than it incentivizes donations among high-value agents and therefore results in a lower aggregate donation rate. When there is excess supply for patients in the first priority tier, both high- and low-value agents are less incentivized under theValue+Donor Priority Rule. It immediately follows that𝜇𝑣⁢𝑠<𝜇𝑠⁢𝑣μv⁢s<μs⁢v. Next, to compare theDonor+Value Priority Ruleand theDonor Priority Rule, we observe that𝑐∗ℎ,𝑠⁢𝑣>𝑐∗ℎ,𝑠ch,s⁢v∗>ch,s∗, whereas low-value agents are less incentivized than their counterparts under theDonor Priority Rulewith𝑐∗𝑙,𝑠⁢𝑣<𝑐∗𝑙,𝑠cl,s⁢v∗<cl,s∗. In aggregate, the second part ofProposition 2establishes that with a concave cost distribution and the gap betweenVhandVlbeing relatively small, the aggregate donation rate under theDonor+Value Priority Ruleis lower than that under theDonor Priority Rule. These conditions essentially ensure that the disincentives for the low-value agents override the incentives for the high-value agents under theDonor+Value Priority Rule. In summary, our theoretical analysis highlights that the efficacy of the dual-incentive priority rules critically depends on the information about individual valuations at the time of donation decisions. In the unobserved value environment, theDonor+Value Priority Rulealways weakly dominates the others. In contrast, if agents know their valuations in advance, theDonor Priority Rulemay strictly dominate the dual-incentive rules.
article.4. Experimental Design and Procedures: Our theoretical results demonstrate the importance of the information environment for the relative performance of the dual-incentive priority rules. To investigate the significance of these differences, we conduct laboratory experiments to compare the donation incentives of subjects under different allocation rules in different information environments. 4.1. States of Nature and Information Following the seminal work ofKessler and Roth (2012), we frame our experiments in neutral terms. That is, we refer to the brain and (other) organs as A and B units, respectively.24In this regard, our experimental design abstracts from the institutional and sentimental details associated with actual organ donation decisions in real life.25 In the experiments, subjects simultaneously and independently decide whether to register as B-unit donors. At the beginning of every round, each subject is endowed with an initial wealth of$⁢3$⁢3: one A unit and one B unit.26Subjects are informed that the A unit will fail with probability 25% and that the B unit will fail with probability 75%. These two shocks are mutually exclusive. The choices of A-unit and B-unit failure rates follow from those inKessler and Roth (2014c)and correspond to a scenario where the maximal production rate of B units is below the demand rate for B units.27Subjects are also informed about the used allocation priority rule of the donated B units. If a subject is a registered donor and encounters A-unit failure, the B unit goes to a common pool of donated organs. This pool of organs is distributed among subjects who have failed B units based on priority ordering. The detailed designs of the allocation priority rules are discussed inSection 4.2. Subjects who register as donors need to pay donation costs. At the beginning of the donation game, each subject is randomly assigned to have a low or high cost of donation. In each group of eight subjects, four subjects belong to the low-cost type and the other four subjects are of the high-cost type.28The low-cost subjects pay$⁢0.5$⁢0.5, whereas the high-cost donors pay$⁢2$⁢2, for their donation decisions. Subjects are informed only of their own cost of donation. After making donation decisions in each round, subjects encounter either A-unit or B-unit failure. When a subject’s B unit fails, the subject needs to receive a B-unit donation, which comes from subjects who encounter A-unit failure and have registered as B-unit donors at the start of that particular round.29Upon receiving a donation, the subject gains either$⁢2$⁢2or$⁢4$⁢4depending on the assigned value in the experimental game.30In each group, there are four low-value subjects and four high-value subjects. Each subject is randomly assigned to have either a low or high value in receiving an organ allocation, which is independent of one’s cost type. If such a subject fails to receive a B-unit allocation, the subject loses$⁢1$⁢1.31 In summary, at the beginning of each round, subjects independently make organ donation decisions. After all the subjects in a group complete their donation decisions, all the donation B units are allocated to those in need based on the priority rules; then, each subject observes one’s own outcome on a computer screen, including the donor status in that particular round, the status of A unit and B unit, whether the subject has received a B-unit donation (in the case of a B-unit failure), and the net earnings obtained in that round of the game. Throughout the experiments, subjects have no direct information about the donation decisions or earnings of the other subjects. 4.2. Experimental Treatments Our experimental environment presents a scenario in which the maximum supply of available organs cannot meet the high-value type’s demand. Essentially, this corresponds to the case of a severe organ shortage in our theoretical framework. This case is more interesting to explore because it closely mimics the global organ shortage, characterized by lengthy waiting lists and a limited supply of transplantable organs. In such a scenario, our theory establishes an equivalence between the two dual-incentive rules, as stated in the first part ofPropositions 1and2.32In our experimental treatments, we focus on the following three allocation rules: theValue Priority Rule, theDonor Priority Rule, and theDonor+Value Priority Rule. (1)Value (Control) Treatment. Subjects are informed that donated B units are first randomly rationed among the high-value subjects who need B units and then (if there are any remaining) among the low-value subjects in a similar manner. (2)Donor Treatment. The donated B units are provided first to those who have encountered B-unit failure and registered as donors through random rationing. Afterward, only if there are any remaining B units will they be provided to those who are not registered donors. Within each donor-status group, B units are assigned randomly. (3)Donor + Value Treatment. This treatment nests theValuetreatment within theDonortreatment. The donated B units are first assigned to those in need according to their donor status and then within the same donor-status group based on their value type. This results in four priority groups, ranked from high to low as high-value donors followed by low-value donors and then high-value nondonors followed by low-value nondonors. Within each priority group, the allocation is performed uniformly and randomly. Following our theoretical framework, we consider two information environments for the experimental treatments, distinguished by the timing of subjects’ receipt of information about their individual valuations for B-unit allocations. In the observed value environment, subjects are informed of their own valuations at the beginning of each round of the game, that is, before making their donation decisions.33In the unobserved value environment, the individual value for organ allocation is revealed only at the end of each round, that is, after the organ allocation process is completed. Accordingly, there are 3 × 2 = 6 different conditions under which subjects make donation decisions, corresponding to the three allocation priority rules and the two information environments. Subjects play the donation game in one of the information environments repeatedly for 30 rounds, which are divided into two parts. In the first half, that is, rounds 1–15, they play the game under one of the three allocation priority rules. In the second half, that is, rounds 16–30, they may play the game under a different allocation priority rule. Subjects are informed whether the rules of the game change at the end of round 15, and any changes in the game are explained in detail. To avoid the end-game effect, we do not tell subjects how many rounds in total they will play the game. Subjects are informed that four rounds are randomly selected to determine their final payments. The combination of between-subject and within-subject designs closely follows the setups ofKessler and Roth (2012),Li et al. (2013,2022),Li (2016), andHerr and Normann (2016,2019), among many others. Table 1summarizes all the experimental treatments and procedures. In each information environment, subjects play theControl(Value) treatment for either the first half or the second half of the rounds. There are nine sessions for each information environment. In each session, 16 subjects split into two groups to form two independent markets under the same treatment. In total, we have 9 × 2 = 18 markets (groups) in each information environment. The total number of subjects in our experiments is2×18×8=2882×18×8=288. These subjects are students from various fields in a major research university in Singapore, recruited through posters and university-level mass email advertisements. Table 1.Summary of the Experimental Treatments and Procedures Table 1.Summary of the Experimental Treatments and Procedures At the beginning of each session, we distribute written instructions to the subjects and read the instructions aloud in front of them. Afterward, the subjects review the instructions on a computer screen and answer four multiple-choice questions related to these instructions. The experiment proceeds to the decision-making stage only when everyone has answered the questions correctly. After completing all experimental rounds, the subjects are asked to complete an online questionnaire containing demographic questions. The average duration of each experimental session is approximately 1.5 hours. At the end of the experiment, all subjects receive a cash payment, including their earnings from four randomly selected rounds and their show-up fee of$⁢2$⁢2. The average total earnings are$⁢12.82$⁢12.82. The experiments are conducted using z-Tree (Fischbacher 2007).
article.5. Experimental Results: Our main interests are to compare the donation rates across the three allocation rules and between the two information environments. This section first analyzes the experimental results in the unobserved value environment, followed by those in the observed value environment. We discuss the results on survival benefits and welfare in Online Appendix F. 5.1. Summary Statistics Figure 1plots the average donation rates at the subject-round level. We observe that in both information environments, the relative rankings between theValuetreatment and theDonortreatment are consistent with those obtained in the literature (Kessler and Roth 2012,Li et al. 2013,Herr and Normann 2016), even though our design parameters differ from those in the previous studies and we further incorporate heterogeneous valuations.34Interestingly, our experiments use a Singapore-based subject pool, whereas the subjects in the previous studies were mostly based in the United States (Kessler and Roth 2012,2014c;Li et al. 2013;Li 2016) or Germany (Herr and Normann 2016,2019). Although the practical organ allocation rule in Singapore is distinct from the rules adopted in the United States and Germany, we obtain comparable results, which suggests consistency of the donor-priority treatment effects across studies. As shown inFigure 1(a), theValuetreatment results in the lowest donation rate of 26.5%, whereas theDonor+Valuetreatment generates the highest donation rate of 54.6% among all three allocation rules in the unobserved value environment. The average donation rate under theDonortreatment is 49.1%, which is 5.5 percentage points lower than that under theDonor+Valuetreatment. These observations provide preliminary evidence of the superior performance of the dual-incentive allocation rule when subjects do not know their value at the time of donation decisions, which is consistent with the prediction inProposition 1. Figure 1(b)presents the results in the observed value environment. We again observe the lowest donation rate of 25.7% under theValuetreatment. The highest donation rate among all three allocation rules of 57.2% is achieved under theDonortreatment condition. TheDonor+Valuetreatment generates an intermediate donation rate of 45.2%. These statistics indicate that introducing the dual-incentive priority rule may be less effective than the single donor-priority incentive if the private value of organ allocation is observed in advance, which provides the first experimental evidence for our prediction inProposition 2. Comparisons between these two sets of results immediately suggest that the relative performance of theDonor Priority Ruleand theDonor+Value Priority Rulecrucially depends on whether the subjects can observe their individual valuations for B-unit allocations when they make their donation decisions. The observed differences are considerable and statistically significant. In the environment where value is unobserved, the dual-incentive treatment results in donation rate increases of 106.3% and 11.3% compared with theValuetreatment and theDonortreatment, respectively, which are statistically significant at the 1% level and the 5% level.35These patterns are more noticeable in the first half but less noticeable in the second half. For example, in rounds 16–30, the average donation rate under the dual-incentive treatment is 56.3%, which, although higher than that in theValuetreatment, barely exceeds the 56.0% under theDonortreatment. This insignificant difference could be due to the overreaction of subjects to the donor-priority incentive amidst the uncertainty surrounding the potential gain of receiving a donation. In contrast, in the observed value environment, the superior performance of theDonor+Value Priority Ruleno longer holds. The donation rate under theDonor+Valuetreatment, although 19.5 percentage points higher than that of theValuetreatment, is 12.0 percentage points lower than that under theDonortreatment. Both these differences are statistically significant at the 1% level and remain robust when the treatment conditions are implemented in either the first or second half of the experiments. To better understand the reversed ranking of the donation rates in the two information environments,Figure 2compares the donation incentives by value type in each information environment. In the unobserved value environment, there is no significant difference in the donation incentives between the high-value and low-value subjects under any of the three priority treatments because each subject believes that one is equally likely to be a high- or low-value type. In contrast, the high-value subjects are significantly more likely to donate than are the low-value subjects under all three priority rules in the observed value environment. Under theValuetreatment, comparisons ofFigure 2, (a) and (b), further suggest that high-value subjects are more incentivized, whereas low-value subjects are less willing to donate when switching from the unobserved to the observed value environment. These differences are significant at the 1% level. Notably, these between- and within-type comparisons depart from our theoretical predictions, which suggest that the type-specific donation rate should beF(0) under theValue Priority Rule; that is, the donations are driven purely by altruistic motives. One reason for these discrepancies may be the type-dependent altruism in the observed value environment, which is not captured by our theoretical model. Specifically, when making a donation decision, subjects incur not only the pecuniary costs assigned in the experiments but also their private warm-glow benefits derived from altruism. The degree of altruism is likely to be contingent on their assigned value type in the observed value environment, with high-value subjects enjoying higher altruistic benefits than low-value subjects.36 InFigure 2(b), we observe that theDonor+Valuetreatment reduces the donation incentives of both high- and low-value subjects in comparison with those under theDonortreatment. This observation differs from our theoretical analysis, which predicts that under theDonor+Value Priority Rule, high (low)-value agents are more (less) incentivized than their counterparts under theDonor Priority Rule.37This could be driven by the overestimation of “priority congestion” under theDonor+Valuetreatment due to the intensified competition for B units among high-value donors, who are exclusively granted the highest priority. Despite these divergences, importantly, the between-type differences inFigure 2provide direct evidence that in the observed value environment, and the incentive distortion between high- and low-value subjects is maximized under the dual-incentiveDonor+Valuetreatment, which consequently results in a lower aggregate donation rate than that under theDonortreatment. Figure 3shows the evolution of donation rates in each round. Similar to many experimental papers on organ donation (Kessler and Roth 2012,2014a;Li et al. 2013,2022;Herr and Normann 2016,2019;Li 2016) and public goods provisions (Ledyard 1995,Chaudhuri 2011), the average donation rate or contribution rate exhibits a declining trend over time.38This pattern is typically driven by the learning and signaling effects and the conditional reciprocity and fairness underlying social preferences (Andreoni 1988,Fehr and Schmidt 1999,Levitt and List 2007). In general, theDonortreatment and theDonor+Valuetreatment outperform theValuetreatment in almost all rounds in both information environments. In the unobserved value environment, theDonor+Valuetreatment generates higher donation rates than theDonortreatment in most rounds. This pattern is more visible in rounds 1–15, as noted in our previous discussion ofFigure 1. When the information environment changes to the observed value treatment, theDonor+Valuetreatment erodes the incentives to donate in comparison with those in theDonortreatment in almost all 30 rounds. These observations again are largely consistent with our theoretical predictions inPropositions 1and2. 5.2. Regression Analysis Table 2presents the estimation results from a linear probability regression model based on the observations in the unobserved value environment.39The dependent variable is whether a subject registers as a donor in each round of the game. In Regression (1), we consider theValuetreatment as our baseline and include two treatment dummy variables:DonorandDonor+Value. The coefficients for both dummy variables are positive and statistically significant. These estimates suggest that under theDonorandDonor+Valuetreatments, subjects are 22.6 and 28.1 percentage points, respectively, more likely to donate than are those in the baseline group. This result remains robust when we add additional controls. Further comparisons of the coefficients ofDonorandDonor+Valueunder all five specifications imply that in the unobserved value environment, theDonor+Valuetreatment substantially outperforms theDonortreatment. The superior performance of theDonor+Value Priority Rulecritically hinges on the unobservability of value at the time of donation decisions, which ensures no incentive distortion between high- and low-value subjects. This result further supports our theoretical prediction inProposition 1. The dummy variableRecipient Last Roundis equal to one if a subject received a donation in the previous round. The estimated coefficient implies that subjects are 15.9 percentage points more likely to donate if they were B-unit recipients in the previous round. Intuitively, the benefit of receiving a B-unit donation can be decomposed into two parts: the positive information on the other subjects’ decisions and the increased earnings from receiving a B unit. To separate these effects, we additionally includeEarnings Last Round, which represents the net earnings in the previous round. Its statistically significant and positive coefficient indicates that previous earnings improve the likelihood of donation in the current round. This finding suggests a potential reciprocity incentive in the sense that subjects hope to contribute to the group because they benefited from the contributions of the other subjects in the previous round. These estimates are also qualitatively similar to those observed in the previous experimental and empirical literature (seeKessler and Roth 2012;Li et al. 2013;Herr and Normann 2016,2019; andLópez et al. 2018, among many others). In Regression (4), we investigate the impacts of subjects’ value type on their donation decisions by including the dummy variableHigh Value. It is equal to one for a subject assigned a high value of$⁢4$⁢4in the experiments. We also interact it with the two treatment dummy variables. Consistent with expectations, none of these coefficients is statistically significant because subjects do not know their individual valuations for B-unit allocations at the time of their donation decisions. The estimates for the other coefficients mostly remain consistent with those from Regression (3) in terms of both the magnitude and statistical significance. We conduct a similar set of regression analyses for the observed value environment and report the results inTable 3. For Regression (1), the estimated coefficients of the treatment dummiesDonorandDonor+Valueindicate that subjects are 31.5 and 19.5 percentage points more likely to register as donors under theDonorandDonor+Valuetreatments, respectively, than under the baselineValuetreatment. These differences are both economically substantial and statistically significant at the 1% level. Furthermore, comparing the coefficients ofDonorandDonor+Value, we find that the donation rate in theDonortreatment is approximately 12 percentage points higher than that under theDonor+Valuetreatment. The estimated coefficients of the other control variables related to donation cost, recipient status, and earnings in the previous round in Regressions (3)–(5) are qualitatively similar to those inTable 2. Comparing these results with those obtained inTable 2, we find a stark difference in terms of the relative performance of theDonor Priority Ruleand theDonor+Value Priority Rule. Although the dual-incentive allocation rule has superior performance in promoting donor registration in an environment where subjects do not observe their individual valuations, it turns out to be less effective than theDonor Priority Rulewhen subjects learn their valuations before making their donation decisions. Therefore, our findings based on theory and experiments highlight a critical role of information in designing a priority allocation rule to incentivize organ donor registration. InTable 3, another critical difference fromTable 2is that the coefficient onHigh Valuebecomes positive and statistically significant at the 1% level in Regressions (4) and (5). Intuitively, in the observed value environment, subjects are informed about their individual values before making their donation decisions; hence, their decisions are contingent on their value types. Specifically, the estimates in Regression (4) suggest that in the baselineValuetreatment, high-value subjects are 25.2 percentage points more likely to donate than are low-value subjects, which could result from the unobserved type-dependent altruism, as discussed inSection 5.1. The positive and statistically significant coefficients on the interaction terms further imply that theDonortreatment and theDonor+Valuetreatment provide even stronger incentives for high-value subjects, which is consistent with our theoretical predictions. The degree of incentive distortions is the highest under theDonor+Value Priority Rule, where the donation rate of high-value subjects is 40.1 percentage points higher than that of low-value subjects. Indeed, as demonstrated in our theoretical analysis, such unbalanced incentives are the main drivers of the underperformance of the dual-incentive allocation rule in the observed value environment. In summary, the regression results inTables 2and3are largely in line with the summary statistics inSection 5.1, as well as our theoretical predictions inSection 3.4. Although our main objective is to investigate the qualitative differences between different priority rules, the observed quantitative results in the controlled laboratory experiments also provide some preliminary predictions about how the actual donation rate may be affected by different policies. Our results indicate that switching from theDonor Priority RuletoDonor+Value Priority Rulein the unobserved value environment or vice versa in the observed value environment can increase the donation rate by approximately 5.24 or 11.98 percentage points, respectively. These increments may create an effective supply of 10,535 or 24,086 deceased donors, which are considerable numbers compared with the 55,124 patients added to the organ waiting list in the United States in 2020.42Nevertheless, an important caveat is that one should interpret these estimates with caution because they are derived based on the specific experimental setup under consideration, which is highly stylized and abstracts away from many practical details.
article.6. Further Discussion and Conclusion: In this study, we examine the significant influence of the information environment in assessing the effectiveness of dual-incentive priority rules for promoting deceased organ donations. The conclusions drawn have substantial implications for enhancing existing policies and devising innovative policy strategies. The success of implementing the donor-priority incentive greatly relies on the current systems, including key characteristics that determine value-based allocation priority and, importantly, the visibility of these characteristics at the time of an individual’s decision to donate. To effectively raise the organ donor registration rate in countries such as the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada, policymakers must thoughtfully assess how the current public information disclosure approach could interact with the proposed donor-priority initiative (Burkell et al. 2013,Gray 2013,Chan 2020). Our findings highlight to a notable extent that creating successful priority-based organ allocation policies requires a combination of market and information design. In contrast, in countries where the donor-priority rule is already implemented, such as Singapore, the main focus shifts to the amount of priority weight that should be assigned to a registered donor in relation to other medical and sociodemographic factors. Our theoretical analysis suggests that refinement of existing policy requires comprehensive examination of the visibility of other attributes impacting waiting list priority and diligent evaluation of the severity of organ shortages. In practice, many scarce resources, such as organs for transplantation, are distributed through priority queues. How the priority queues are administered affects the scarcity of the organs by influencing the private provision incentives and modifying allocation efficiencies. Organ donations from individuals contribute to a public pool available to potential recipients, mirroring the private provision of public goods. However, each organ transplant is unique to a single patient, making an organ for transplantation an ex ante public good and an ex post private good (Kessler and Roth 2012,2014b). Thus, this study broadly enhances our comprehension of motivation in the private provision of ex ante public goods and the best allocation rules for ex post private goods. Our paper’s theoretical model and experimental design can be extended into various dimensions to account for more institutional and sentiment details that influence actual donation behavior.43First, although our theoretical analysis indicates that regardless of the different information environment, theDonor+Value Priority Ruleand theValue+Donor Priority Ruleare equivalent in a scenario of severe organ shortage, it is interesting to compare these two dual-incentive priority rules in different information environments in the experiment to provide further insights and guidance for practical policy design. In view of the ethical challenges related to the donor-priority incentive (Lavee and Brock 2012,Quigley et al. 2012,Berzon 2018), theValue+Donor Priority Ruleis likely to be more acceptable and less controversial in practical implementation because it aligns more closely with the preexisting rules and arrangements in most countries. Another immediate extension of the experiments is to design a scenario with a moderate shortage and explore the limitations of the parameter assumptions imposed in our theoretical analysis. This can generate more interesting comparisons between the two dual-incentive priority rules in different information environments and in alternative market scenarios, thereby facilitating more comprehensive comparisons of the different priority rules. Second, the value-based priority rules considered in our study represent reduced formulations of the practical policies because, based on the interpretations inSection 2.1, value broadly captures several observable or unobservable elements that determine a candidate’s priority on the organ waiting list. Future investigations on the actual value perceived by individuals and the information available to them when making donation decisions, such as the awareness of the allocation priority rules and the likelihood of health shocks, are useful to thoroughly understand the tradeoffs faced by individuals from various perspectives.44Following the approach ofElias et al. (2015)andHouse et al. (2022), we could conduct survey or field experiments among the general public or related professionals, which will provide important foundations and valuable insights to ensure that new policy recommendations achieve the desired results when put into practical implementation. Third, with the current setup, we can introduce priority rules that yield probabilistic instead of deterministic increments in the priority queues, that is, relative priority for donors. Although the allocation rules considered in this paper are lexicographic to any other factors, many practical organ allocation mechanisms are point based, where patients receive priority points based on several criteria, including waiting time, medial urgency, geographic location, and others. Accordingly, with the donor-priority incentive, a registered donor does not necessarily have an absolutely higher priority than that of all nonregistered donors. Under a more general family of point-based priority rules, which assign a relatively higher priority to donors based on some additional priority points, the key tradeoffs and intuitions underlying our analysis continue to hold, at least qualitatively. This is because under the donor status–based priority rules, the marginal benefits of donation still hinge on the difference in the probability of receiving a donation between donors and nondonors, the value type, and the information environment. As a result, the relative ranking of the different priority rules remains unchanged, in particular, granting more priority to the donors can generate higher donation rates. Indeed, recent studies have shown that giving more priority to registered donors leads to higher registration rates in the laboratory (Herr and Normann 2019). Similarly, future experimental studies may perform comparative statics analysis of the dual-incentive allocation rules by varying the degree of relative priority. Finally, in terms of the modeling approach, our theoretical framework considers two extreme information environments. The intermediate case with partially observed value, where the value of organ allocation is known to some individuals but not others, deserves formal investigation in future research. Intuitively, this situation would generate results that lie between the outcomes of the two extremes that we have analyzed. The performance of the dual-incentive priority rules relative to theDonor Priority Ruleis likely to depend on the proportion of informed individuals in the population. Because our theoretical analysis in the observed value environment is based on a set of sufficient assumptions, another related direction that warrants future investigation is to relax these restrictive assumptions to derive necessary and sufficient conditions on the relative performance of the different priority rules. Moreover, it is important to compare these parameter assumptions with the empirical data in different countries to better inform practical policy design.
